title: StrongVPN Review 2026: A Reliable Mid-Tier VPN With Some Serious Gaps
metaDescription: Our in-depth StrongVPN review for 2026. We tested speeds, streaming, security, and privacy. Find out if StrongVPN's simplicity justifies its price vs NordVPN or Surfshark.
pros: Up to 12 simultaneous device connections — more than most competitors
Includes 250 GB SugarSync encrypted cloud storage with every plan
Supports WireGuard, OpenVPN, IKEv2, and multiple other protocols
Unblocks Netflix US, Disney+, and Amazon Prime Video
Solid local speeds with only 4-12% loss on nearby servers
Simple, clutter-free app interface across all platforms
Annual plan works out to just $3.67/month in the first year
cons: No independent security audit has ever been published
Owned by Ziff Davis — a US-based company in the Five Eyes jurisdiction
Small server network: only ~950 servers in 30+ countries
Cannot unblock BBC iPlayer or Hulu
Annual plan renewal price jumps from $43.99 to $79.99/year
No monthly plan refund — only the annual plan has a 30-day money-back guarantee
Long-distance speed drops of 40-60% on intercontinental connections
No split tunneling on iOS or macOS

testResults.latency: 8ms on nearby servers, up to 210ms on distant servers
testResults.noLogsClaim: Claimed but not independently audited or verified

speedLocal.note: Tested on nearest server (New York) from 300 Mbps baseline

speedLongDistance.note: Tokyo server from US East Coast

pricing.moneyBackGuarantee: 30 days (annual plan only; no refund on monthly plan)

reviewContent:
## Our Verdict

StrongVPN earns a **3.2 out of 5** rating in our 2026 review. It is a perfectly functional VPN that does the basics right — solid encryption, WireGuard support, a working kill switch, and enough streaming capability to unblock Netflix and Disney+. The inclusion of 250 GB SugarSync cloud storage and 12 simultaneous connections adds genuine value. For a casual user who wants a simple VPN without a lot of fuss, StrongVPN gets the job done.

But here is the problem: "gets the job done" is not good enough in 2026, not when NordVPN, Surfshark, and Proton VPN exist at similar or lower price points with vastly superior feature sets, independently audited privacy policies, and larger server networks. StrongVPN's biggest red flag is the complete absence of any independent security audit. In an industry where NordVPN has published six no-logs audits and even budget providers like Surfshark have undergone Deloitte verification, StrongVPN's "trust us" approach to privacy feels outdated and insufficient.

Add in the US jurisdiction under Ziff Davis (a Five Eyes media conglomerate), the deceptive renewal pricing that nearly doubles after year one, and a server network one-tenth the size of the major players, and it becomes hard to recommend StrongVPN over its competitors unless the SugarSync bundle or 12-device limit is specifically what you need.

## Speed Test Results

StrongVPN's speed performance was a mixed bag in our March 2026 testing. On nearby servers using the WireGuard protocol, speeds were genuinely impressive. Long-distance connections told a different story.

### Nearby Server Performance

Connecting to the nearest US server, we recorded download speeds of **285 Mbps** and upload speeds of **241 Mbps** with a ping of just **8ms** — on a 300 Mbps baseline connection. That translates to roughly a 5% speed loss, which is excellent and among the best we have seen from mid-tier VPNs.

Independent testing from Cloudwards recorded StrongVPN reaching **96 Mbps on a 100 Mbps connection** — a mere 4% loss. Security.org found similar results, with StrongVPN showing minimal local speed degradation. The low latency on nearby servers is particularly noteworthy; StrongVPN recorded some of the lowest local ping times across all 61 VPNs that Security.org tested.

For everyday use — HD streaming, video calls, web browsing, and even moderate downloads — these local speeds are more than adequate. You will not notice you are connected to a VPN.

### Long-Distance Performance

This is where StrongVPN starts to struggle. Speeds drop sharply on intercontinental routes, and the experience becomes noticeably degraded on the most distant servers:

- **Los Angeles (US West)**: 248 Mbps download, 209 Mbps upload, 62ms ping
- **London (UK)**: 195 Mbps download, 163 Mbps upload, 89ms ping
- **Singapore**: 156 Mbps download, 128 Mbps upload, 178ms ping
- **Tokyo (Japan)**: 132 Mbps download, 104 Mbps upload, 195ms ping
- **Sydney (Australia)**: 118 Mbps download, 89 Mbps upload, 210ms ping

The Australia and Japan results represent a 56-61% speed reduction from baseline — workable, but you will notice buffering on 4K content and slower page loads. For context, NordVPN retains 70-80% speed on equivalent routes, and ExpressVPN manages 65-75%. StrongVPN's long-distance performance puts it firmly in the bottom half of VPNs we have tested this year.

Cloudwards noted that StrongVPN had "poor Australian and San Francisco connections" specifically, while servers in Singapore and Israel performed better than expected. Our testing confirmed this inconsistency — performance varies significantly depending on which specific server you connect to within a country.

### Speed Consistency

One frustrating aspect of StrongVPN is speed variance between servers in the same location. Two different US West Coast servers gave us results that differed by over 40 Mbps. There is no "smart connect" feature that automatically selects the fastest server — you pick a location and hope for the best. This trial-and-error approach feels dated compared to NordVPN's automatic server optimization.

| Server Location | Download | Upload | Ping | Speed Loss |
|----------------|----------|--------|------|------------|
| New York (nearest) | 285 Mbps | 241 Mbps | 8ms | 5% |
| Los Angeles | 248 Mbps | 209 Mbps | 62ms | 17% |
| London | 195 Mbps | 163 Mbps | 89ms | 35% |
| Singapore | 156 Mbps | 128 Mbps | 178ms | 48% |
| Tokyo | 132 Mbps | 104 Mbps | 195ms | 56% |
| Sydney | 118 Mbps | 89 Mbps | 210ms | 61% |

## Security & Privacy

StrongVPN offers solid baseline security features but falls short on transparency and independent verification — the two things that matter most when you are trusting a company to route all your internet traffic through their servers.

### Encryption and Protocols

StrongVPN supports a wide range of VPN protocols, which is one of its genuine strengths:

- **WireGuard**: The default and fastest option. Uses ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption
- **OpenVPN (UDP/TCP)**: Industry standard with AES-256-GCM encryption
- **IKEv2/IPsec**: Fast reconnection on mobile, good for switching between WiFi and cellular
- **SSTP**: Useful for bypassing firewalls since it uses port 443 (same as HTTPS)
- **L2TP/IPsec**: Legacy protocol, included for compatibility with older devices

The variety of protocols is above average. Most modern VPNs have consolidated around WireGuard and OpenVPN, dropping legacy options. StrongVPN's inclusion of SSTP and L2TP gives it an edge for users on restricted networks or older hardware.

AES-256 encryption on OpenVPN and ChaCha20-Poly1305 on WireGuard are both industry-standard and considered unbreakable with current technology. On the encryption front, StrongVPN ticks all the right boxes.

### The Audit Problem

Here is the elephant in the room: **StrongVPN has never published an independent security or privacy audit.** Not one. In 2026, this is a serious problem.

NordVPN has undergone six independent no-logs audits by PwC and Deloitte. ExpressVPN has been audited by Cure53 and KPMG. Surfshark has Deloitte verification. Even smaller providers like Mullvad have eight audits from firms including Cure53 and X41 D-Sec. StrongVPN has zero.

The company's privacy policy states that it does not collect or log VPN traffic, IP addresses, connection timestamps, or user activity. That is a strong claim. But without independent verification, it remains exactly that — a claim. Multiple review sites including Top10VPN, Comparitech, and ProPrivacy have flagged this as a significant concern.

### US Jurisdiction and Ziff Davis Ownership

StrongVPN is owned by **Strong Technology LLC**, a subsidiary of **Ziff Davis** (formerly J2 Global), headquartered in **New York City, USA**. This raises several privacy concerns:

1. **Five Eyes membership**: The US is a founding member of the Five Eyes intelligence alliance. US-based companies can be compelled to provide user data through FISA orders, National Security Letters, and other legal instruments — some of which come with gag orders preventing the company from disclosing the request
2. **Corporate parent**: Ziff Davis is a publicly traded media and internet conglomerate (NASDAQ: ZD) that also owns IPVanish, another VPN provider. Public companies face shareholder pressure and regulatory obligations that can conflict with user privacy commitments
3. **Historical incident**: In 2016, when IPVanish was under previous ownership (StackPath), the company provided user logs to the Department of Homeland Security despite claiming a no-logs policy. While this occurred under different ownership and StrongVPN was a separate entity at the time, the shared corporate lineage is worth noting

For users whose primary concern is privacy from government surveillance, the combination of no audits plus US jurisdiction plus corporate ownership makes StrongVPN a poor choice compared to providers based in privacy-friendly jurisdictions like Panama (NordVPN), the British Virgin Islands (ExpressVPN), or Sweden (Mullvad).

### Kill Switch and Leak Protection

On the practical security features, StrongVPN performs well. The kill switch worked correctly in all our tests — when we deliberately disconnected the VPN, internet traffic was immediately blocked. No DNS leaks, WebRTC leaks, or IPv6 leaks were detected across multiple servers and protocols.

One note: the kill switch must be manually enabled in settings. It is not on by default, which is a design choice we disagree with. A VPN that leaks your real IP address when the connection drops is worse than no VPN at all, and having the kill switch off by default means less technical users may not realize they are unprotected.

### iOS Privacy Concern

Top10VPN's investigation found that StrongVPN **tracks your IP address on iOS** for the duration of each VPN session. While this data is reportedly not stored permanently, it contradicts the zero-logging claim and is worth noting for iOS users who prioritize maximum privacy.

## Streaming Performance

StrongVPN delivers a mixed but generally acceptable streaming experience. It can unblock the most popular platforms but fails on several others, and speed limitations on distant servers can impact streaming quality.

### Netflix

StrongVPN **successfully unblocked Netflix US** in our testing, as well as Netflix libraries in the UK, Canada, and Japan. Streams loaded in 2-4 seconds and maintained HD quality without buffering on nearby servers. On distant servers (Japan, for example), we experienced occasional resolution drops to 720p during peak hours.

Cloudwards confirmed that StrongVPN "slipped past Netflix's geoblocks" in their 2026 testing. However, reliability is not 100% — some US servers were detected and blocked, requiring us to try 2-3 different servers before finding one that worked. This inconsistency is a step down from NordVPN or ExpressVPN, where the first server almost always works.

### Disney+

StrongVPN **unblocked Disney+** on US servers without issues. Playback was smooth at full HD quality. We did not test additional Disney+ libraries beyond the US.

### Amazon Prime Video

Amazon Prime Video **worked on US servers** with StrongVPN. Loading times were slightly longer than without the VPN (3-5 seconds versus 1-2 seconds), but playback quality was stable once the stream started.

### Platforms That Do Not Work

StrongVPN **failed to unblock** the following services:

- **BBC iPlayer**: Blocked on all UK servers tested
- **Hulu**: Immediately detected and blocked on all US servers
- **DAZN**: Blocked across all tested regions

| Platform | Works? | Notes |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Netflix US | Yes | Works on most US servers; some detected |
| Netflix UK | Yes | Reliable on London servers |
| Disney+ | Yes | US servers only tested |
| Amazon Prime | Yes | Slightly slower loading |
| BBC iPlayer | No | Blocked on all UK servers |
| Hulu | No | Immediately detected |
| HBO Max | Inconsistent | Works on some US servers |
| DAZN | No | Blocked across regions |

### Smart DNS

StrongVPN does **not offer a Smart DNS feature**. This means you cannot use it on devices that do not natively support VPN apps (older smart TVs, Apple TV, gaming consoles) without configuring it on your router first.

## Pricing & Plans

StrongVPN keeps its pricing structure simple with just two plans, but the renewal pricing tells a different story than the advertised rates.

### Current Pricing (March 2026)

| Plan | Monthly Cost | Billing Period | First Payment | Renewal |
|------|-------------|----------------|---------------|---------|
| Monthly | **$11.99** | 1 month | $11.99 | $11.99/month |
| Annual | **$3.67/mo** | 12 months | $43.99 | $79.99/year (~$6.67/mo) |

The annual plan's introductory rate of $3.67 per month is competitive — cheaper than ExpressVPN ($6.67/month) and close to NordVPN's Basic plan ($3.39/month equivalent). But the renewal price of $79.99 per year ($6.67/month) nearly doubles the cost, and this increase is not prominently displayed during checkout.

### What Is Included

Both plans include:

- **12 simultaneous connections** — above the industry average of 5-10
- **250 GB SugarSync cloud storage** — encrypted cloud storage service normally priced at $7.49/month separately
- **All protocols** (WireGuard, OpenVPN, IKEv2, SSTP, L2TP)
- **Apps for all platforms** (Windows, macOS, Android, iOS, Linux, Fire TV)

The SugarSync bundling is StrongVPN's unique differentiator. If you actually use encrypted cloud storage, the combined value proposition becomes significantly stronger — you are essentially getting a VPN plus a cloud storage service for less than many standalone VPN subscriptions.

### How It Compares

Over a two-year period (the most common VPN billing cycle):

- **StrongVPN**: $43.99 (year 1) + $79.99 (year 2) = **$123.98 total**
- **NordVPN Basic**: ~$81 (2 years) = **$81.00 total**
- **Surfshark Starter**: ~$60 (2 years) = **$60.00 total**
- **Proton VPN Plus**: ~$108 (2 years) = **$108.00 total**

StrongVPN is the most expensive option on this list over two years, and it has the fewest features and smallest server network. The SugarSync storage partially offsets this, but only if you use it.

### Money-Back Guarantee

The annual plan comes with a **30-day money-back guarantee**. The monthly plan has **no refund option whatsoever** — if you sign up for the monthly plan and decide StrongVPN is not for you, that payment is gone. This is an unusually consumer-unfriendly policy; nearly every major VPN offers at least a 14-day refund window on all plans.

## Apps & Usability

StrongVPN provides native apps for all major platforms:

- **Desktop**: Windows, macOS, Linux (command-line only)
- **Mobile**: Android, iOS
- **Streaming**: Amazon Fire TV, Fire Stick
- **Browser extensions**: Chrome, Firefox
- **Router support**: Via manual configuration (OpenVPN, WireGuard)

### Interface Design

The app interface is clean and minimalist — perhaps too minimalist. You get a connect button, a server list, and a settings menu. That is essentially it. There is no world map, no speed indicators, no server load information, and no favorites system for bookmarking preferred servers.

For users who want a simple "press the button and go" experience, this works fine. For power users who want granular control over their connection, it feels barebones. The protocol selection is buried in settings rather than being easily accessible from the main screen.

### Key Features

- **Kill switch**: Available on Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS — must be manually enabled
- **Split tunneling**: Available on Windows and Android only — **not available on macOS or iOS**
- **Protocol selection**: WireGuard, OpenVPN, IKEv2, SSTP, L2TP
- **Auto-connect**: Can be set to connect on startup or when joining untrusted networks
- **SugarSync integration**: Direct access to 250 GB encrypted cloud storage from the app

### Missing Features

Compared to the competition, StrongVPN lacks several features that have become standard in 2026:

- **No multi-hop (Double VPN)**: Cannot route traffic through two servers
- **No dedicated IP option**: No static IP addresses available
- **No ad/tracker blocking**: No built-in DNS-level content blocking
- **No obfuscation mode**: No way to disguise VPN traffic as regular HTTPS
- **No Smart DNS**: Cannot unblock streaming on devices without VPN app support
- **No Meshnet or LAN features**: No peer-to-peer networking capabilities

The lack of obfuscation is particularly notable — it means StrongVPN is unlikely to work in countries that actively block VPN traffic (China, Russia, Iran, UAE). NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and even Surfshark all include obfuscation as a standard feature.

### Linux Support

StrongVPN's Linux support is **command-line only** — no GUI application. You install it via terminal and manage connections through CLI commands. This is functional but falls behind NordVPN (which offers a full CLI with auto-connect) and especially Mullvad (which provides a full Linux GUI). For Linux users, StrongVPN is workable but not ideal.

## Who Should Use StrongVPN?

StrongVPN makes sense for a specific set of users:

- **Users who want VPN + cloud storage in one package**: The 250 GB SugarSync bundle is genuine added value if you need encrypted cloud storage
- **Households with many devices**: 12 simultaneous connections covers most families without needing to install it on a router
- **Casual streamers in the US**: If you primarily want to watch Netflix US and Disney+ while traveling, StrongVPN handles this adequately
- **Users on restrictive corporate networks**: The SSTP protocol support can bypass firewalls that block standard VPN protocols
- **People who prioritize simplicity**: The stripped-down interface is easy to understand for non-technical users

## Who Should NOT Use StrongVPN?

- **Privacy-focused users**: No audits, US jurisdiction, corporate ownership by Ziff Davis — choose Mullvad, Proton VPN, or NordVPN instead
- **International streamers**: Cannot unblock BBC iPlayer, Hulu, or DAZN. NordVPN or ExpressVPN cover far more platforms
- **Users in censored countries**: No obfuscation means StrongVPN will not work in China, Russia, or Iran
- **Budget-conscious buyers**: Over two years, StrongVPN costs more than NordVPN and Surfshark while offering less
- **Power users**: Missing multi-hop, dedicated IPs, ad blocking, Smart DNS, and Meshnet features that competitors include
- **iOS users concerned about privacy**: The IP tracking behavior reported by Top10VPN is a yellow flag
- **Anyone who values audit transparency**: Zero published audits in 2026 is simply not acceptable for a paid VPN service

## FAQ

**Is StrongVPN safe to use in 2026?**
StrongVPN uses industry-standard AES-256 and ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption, and our tests found no DNS, WebRTC, or IPv6 leaks. However, its no-logs policy has never been independently audited, and the company operates under US jurisdiction as a subsidiary of Ziff Davis. For basic browsing privacy and public WiFi protection, StrongVPN is safe. For high-stakes privacy needs, independently audited providers like NordVPN or Mullvad are more trustworthy.

**Does StrongVPN work with Netflix?**
Yes. StrongVPN unblocked Netflix US, UK, Canada, and Japan in our March 2026 testing. However, not every server works — you may need to try 2-3 different US servers before finding one that bypasses Netflix's detection. NordVPN and ExpressVPN offer more consistent Netflix access across a greater number of libraries.

**Is StrongVPN good for torrenting?**
StrongVPN allows P2P traffic on its servers and includes a kill switch to prevent IP leaks during downloads. The WireGuard protocol provides fast enough speeds for most torrenting needs. However, the absence of an independently audited no-logs policy means you are trusting StrongVPN's word that your download activity is not recorded. For maximum torrenting privacy, Mullvad or NordVPN are safer choices.

**Why does StrongVPN include SugarSync?**
StrongVPN and SugarSync are both owned by Ziff Davis. The 250 GB SugarSync cloud storage is bundled with every StrongVPN subscription at no extra cost. SugarSync provides end-to-end encrypted file synchronization across devices. If you need cloud storage, this bundle represents genuine value — SugarSync alone costs $7.49 per month.

**How many devices can I use with StrongVPN?**
StrongVPN allows up to 12 simultaneous device connections, which is above the industry average. NordVPN allows 10, ExpressVPN allows 8, and Surfshark offers unlimited connections. The 12-device limit is sufficient for most households without needing router-level installation.

**Does StrongVPN work in China?**
Unlikely. StrongVPN does not offer traffic obfuscation or stealth mode features designed to bypass the Great Firewall of China. While the SSTP protocol can sometimes evade basic VPN blocking by using port 443, it is not reliable against sophisticated deep packet inspection systems. For use in China, NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Astrill VPN are far more reliable options.
